Medical Appointment No-Show — EDA Report

Dataset: data/kaggle.csv  |  Generated: 2026-03-17  |  Rows after cleaning: 110,521

metric value

Neighbourhood with lowest no-show rate (min 50 appts) MÁRIO CYPRESTE (14.6%)

Dirty rows removed: 6 (saved to dirty.csv with reason column)

2. Key Findings

  • The overall no-show rate is ~20.2%, meaning roughly 1 in 5 appointments is missed.
  • Longer lead times strongly predict no-shows: same-day appointments have the lowest no-show rate; appointments booked 31+ days ahead have the highest.
  • Despite intuition, patients who received an SMS reminder had a higher no-show rate. This is a selection-bias artefact: SMS reminders were disproportionately sent for high-lead-time appointments.
  • Younger patients (11–30 age group) have higher no-show rates; elderly patients (60+) are among the most reliable attenders.
  • Welfare-programme (Scholarship) recipients have a slightly higher no-show rate, likely reflecting transport or employment constraints.
  • Day of week matters: Friday appointments have the highest no-show rate; Tuesday and Wednesday appointments are most reliably attended.
  • Substantial neighbourhood-level variation exists; high-volume central neighbourhoods tend to have lower no-show rates.
  • Patients with a history of missing appointments are significantly more likely to miss their next appointment — prior behaviour is the strongest single predictor.

6. Hard Interview Questions — Q&A

Click each question to expand the answer.

Q: Why does SMS_received correlate positively with no-shows, when you'd expect reminders to help?
SMS reminders appear counterproductive at first glance, but this is a selection-bias artefact. Reminders were disproportionately sent to patients with longer lead-time appointments. Since lead time is the dominant predictor of no-show, patients who received SMS are already at higher baseline risk. When you control for lead time (see Plot 19 — SMS Paradox), the SMS effect is reduced or reversed within each lead-time bucket. The lesson: never interpret a raw correlation without considering confounders.
Q: What is the effect of appointment lead time on attendance?
Lead time shows a near-monotonic relationship with no-show rate. Same-day appointments have the lowest no-show rates (patients are motivated and remember their appointment). The rate rises steadily through 1–7 days, 8–30 days, 31–90 days, reaching 30%+ for appointments booked more than 90 days ahead. Practically, this suggests scheduling systems should minimise lead times where possible, and should prioritise reminder systems for long-lead appointments.
Q: Which patient segments are at highest risk of not attending?
The highest-risk segments are:
  • Young adults (11–30): highest no-show rate across age groups
  • Long lead-time appointments (31d+): strong monotonic effect
  • Patients with prior no-show history: strongest single predictor — past behaviour predicts future behaviour
  • Scholarship (welfare) recipients: slightly elevated rate, likely socioeconomic barriers
  • Friday appointments: highest no-show rate by day of week
  • Certain neighbourhoods: substantial geographic variation in no-show rate
Q: How would you build a no-show prediction model from this data?
Target: Binary classification — noshow_flag (1 = missed).
Features: lead_days, prior_noshow_rate (computed from patient history), age, appointment_dow, handcap_binary, Scholarship, Hipertension, Diabetes, neighbourhood (encoded).
Class imbalance (80/20): Use class_weight='balanced' or SMOTE; evaluate with AUROC (not accuracy, which is misleading on imbalanced data).
Algorithm: Start with logistic regression as interpretable baseline; then gradient boosting (XGBoost/LightGBM) for performance.
Cross-validation: Time-based split (train on earlier months, test on later) to avoid data leakage — never use random splits when temporal order matters.
Leakage risk: prior_noshow_rate must be computed using only past appointments, not future ones.
Q: What are the ethical considerations of using neighbourhood or scholarship status as features?
These features are proxies for socioeconomic status and potentially ethnicity. Using them in an automated system risks:
  • Proxy discrimination: deprioritising patients from poorer neighbourhoods who may already have worse health access
  • Feedback loops: a model trained on biased outcomes may perpetuate existing inequalities
  • Regulatory risk: Brazil's LGPD (Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados) and general data protection principles require fairness considerations
Mitigations: audit for demographic parity, use fairness-aware ML, ensure human oversight of automated decisions, and consider whether features improve individual or only aggregate predictions.
Q: What are the limitations of this dataset?
  • Geographic scope: Single city (Vitória, ES, Brazil) — results may not generalise
  • Temporal scope: ~7 months (Nov 2015 – Jun 2016) — no seasonal patterns, no trend analysis
  • No reason for no-show: We can predict but not explain — patients may have rescheduled, recovered, or had emergencies
  • No appointment type: Specialty, urgency, and provider are not captured; these likely matter significantly
  • PatientId precision issue: Stored as float64 — possible precision loss for large IDs affecting patient-level analysis
  • No socioeconomic depth: Neighbourhood is a coarse proxy; income, transport access, and employment are not available
  • Handcap column: Multi-level (0–4) but treated as binary due to tiny sample sizes at levels 2–4
